title: Undescended Testicle in Puppies (Cryptorchidism) - Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn about cryptorchidism in puppies, why an undescended testicle is a low-urgency concern, and how photos help your vet plan the best care.
content_html:
What is an Undescended Testicle?

An undescended testicle, medically known as cryptorchidism, occurs when one or both of a puppy's testicles fail to move down into the scrotum. Normally, testicles descend from the abdomen into the scrotum by the time a puppy is two months old. If they haven't appeared by six months, the condition is considered permanent.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for an undescended testicle is Low. This is not a life-threatening condition that requires immediate after-hours care. However, it is an issue that must be addressed during a scheduled veterinary appointment. While not an immediate danger, leaving a testicle inside the body long-term increases the risk of testicular cancer and a painful condition called testicular torsion later in life.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a clear photo of your puppy's inguinal (groin) area can be very helpful for your veterinary team. A visual allows the vet to see if there is any swelling or if the testicle is 'prescrotal' (just under the skin near the scrotum) versus being completely hidden inside the abdomen. This information helps the surgical team plan for a more efficient neutering procedure, as the location of the testicle determines where the incisions will be made.

faqs.question: Can the testicle still drop on its own?
faqs.answer: In most puppies, the testicles descend by 8 weeks of age. While there is a small chance they may drop up until 6 months of age, it is very unlikely to happen after that point.

faqs.question: Does my puppy need surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes, veterinarians strongly recommend neutering dogs with cryptorchidism. The retained testicle is at a significantly higher risk for developing tumors due to the higher temperature inside the body compared to the scrotum.

faqs.question: Is cryptorchidism hereditary?
faqs.answer: Yes, this is a genetic trait. Because it can be passed down to offspring, dogs with undescended testicles should not be used for breeding.
